73	## 1. Category Taxonomy
74	
75	Every state-machine name observed in DDx falls into exactly one of the
76	following categories. The category determines where the name is allowed to
77	live (schema, label namespace, event kind, queue derivation, or worker
78	process state).
79	
80	| Category | Storage location | Lifecycle | Owner |
81	|---|---|---|---|
82	| Persisted bead status | `status` field on the bead record | Mutated by atomic snapshot rewrite | Bead store, locked to bd/br set |
83	| Derived queue category | Computed on read from status + labels + deps | Never persisted | Queue derivation code (`ddx bead ready/blocked/status`) |
84	| Event kind | Append-only entry in `Extra["events"][].kind` | Append-only | Drain loop, agent service, CLI |
85	| Terminal phase | A persisted `closed` status plus a closing event/label that names *why* | Mutated once on close | Drain loop / CLI |
86	| Claim metadata | `assignee`, `claimed-at`, `claimed-pid` fields (preserved extras) | Set on claim, cleared on unclaim, expired by triage | Claim resolution path (TD-004) |
87	| Label | Entry in the `labels` array | Add/remove during normal mutation | Anyone with `ddx bead update` |
88	| Extra metadata field | Arbitrary key under preserved extras | Free-form per consumer | Subsystem owning that key |
89	| Worker state | In-memory state of the drain process | Lives only for the worker's lifetime | Drain loop process |
90	
91	A name MUST NOT span categories. If a name today appears as both a label and
92	a derived queue category (e.g., `ready` is derived; `ready` MUST NOT also
93	appear as a label), the sibling reconciliation bead removes the duplicate
94	usage.
95	
96	## 2. Persisted Status Enumeration
97	
98	The persisted bead status is exactly:
99	
100	```
101	open | in_progress | closed | blocked | proposed | cancelled
102	```
103	
104	This is the bd/br-canonical set; it matches `bead-record.schema.json`. This
105	TD does **not** add, rename, or remove any persisted status.
106	
107	Plain-English semantics:
108	
109	- `open` — the bead exists and is eligible for queue derivation. No claim is
110	  active.
111	- `in_progress` — the bead has an active claim (`assignee`, `claimed-at`,
112	  `claimed-pid` populated). The drain loop or an operator has taken
113	  ownership.
114	- `closed` — the bead is terminal. The reason for closure is encoded in a
115	  closing event and/or label; the status itself does not carry the reason.
116	- `blocked` — the bead cannot progress because of a hard precondition (an
117	  upstream dependency, an external blocker). The reason is encoded in a
118	  label (`blocked-on-upstream:<id>`) or an event.
119	- `proposed` — the bead is captured but has not been accepted into the
120	  active queue. Used for triage backlogs; not consumed by drain.
121	- `cancelled` — the bead was abandoned without completion. Distinct from
122	  `closed`: `cancelled` means "we are not doing this work"; `closed` means
123	  "the work is done (or determined unnecessary)".
124	
125	## 3. Transition Matrix
126	
127	| From → To | Allowed? | Driver | Event fired |
128	|---|---|---|---|
129	| `proposed` → `open` | yes | operator (`ddx bead update --status open`) | `triaged` |
130	| `proposed` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
131	| `open` → `in_progress` | yes | drain loop or operator (claim) | `claimed` |
132	| `open` → `blocked` | yes | operator or auto-triage | `blocked` |
133	| `open` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
134	| `in_progress` → `open` | yes | unclaim (operator or stale-claim sweep) | `unclaimed` |
135	| `in_progress` → `closed` | yes | drain loop (on merge/already-satisfied) or operator | `closed-merged` / `closed-already-satisfied` |
136	| `in_progress` → `blocked` | yes | drain loop on non-automatable review/readiness finding, or operator | `review-block` / `blocked` |
137	| `blocked` → `open` | yes | operator (block resolved) | `unblocked` |
138	| `blocked` → `cancelled` | yes | operator | `cancelled` |
139	| `closed` → * | no | — | — (closed is terminal) |
140	| `cancelled` → * | no | — | — (cancelled is terminal) |
141	| any → `proposed` | no | — | proposed is an entry-only status |
142	
143	Closed and cancelled are terminal. Re-opening a closed bead is not a
144	transition; it is filing a follow-up bead with `replaces` set.
145	
146	## 4. Event Vocabulary (unified)
147	
148	Events are append-only entries on `Extra["events"]` (see TD-004 evidence
149	model). The kinds below form the closed vocabulary used across the five
150	hygiene beads. New kinds require updating this section.
151	
152	Lifecycle events:
153	
154	- `triaged` — `proposed → open`
155	- `claimed` — claim acquired
156	- `unclaimed` — claim released
157	- `blocked` — moved to `blocked` (carries a reason in `body`)
158	- `unblocked` — moved off `blocked`
159	- `closed-merged` — drain loop closed after a merge
160	- `closed-already-satisfied` — drain loop detected the work was already done
161	- `closed` — operator-driven close (catch-all)
162	- `cancelled` — moved to `cancelled`
163	
164	Drain-outcome events (no_changes family — NoChangesContract):
165	
166	- `no_changes_verified` — the attempt produced no commit, but supplied a
167	  `verification_command` that passed; the bead is already satisfied.
168	- `no_changes_unverified` — the attempt produced no commit and supplied a
169	  `verification_command`, but the command failed or could not run.
170	- `no_changes_unjustified` — the attempt produced no commit without enough
171	  structured rationale to prove either satisfaction or a durable blocker.
172	- `no_changes_needs_investigation` — the attempt explicitly asked for operator
173	  triage with `status: needs_investigation`.
174	- `no_changes_decomposed` — agent decomposed the bead instead of changing
175	  files.
176	- `no_changes_blocked` — agent declared no_changes with a justified external
177	  blocker.
178	- `no_changes_recoverable` — transient cause; retrying the same bead after
179	  time passes can plausibly succeed.
180	
181	Drain-control events:
182	
183	- `drain-paused-quota` — QuotaPauseContract: drain paused because the active
184	  harness reported quota exhaustion
185	- `drain-resumed-quota`
186	- `rate-limit-retry` — RateLimitRetryContract: a single attempt was retried
187	  after a rate-limit response
188	- `lock-contention` — LockContentionContract: store-lock contention was
189	  observed and handled (retry or backoff)
190	
191	Review and triage events:
192	
193	- `review-block` — reviewer raised a BLOCKING finding
194	- `review-pass` — reviewer cleared the change
195	- `review-request-changes` — one or more reviewer slots returned a structured
196	  `REQUEST_CHANGES` verdict; body carries the review group id and per-AC findings
197	- `review-fixable-gap` — reviewer found an implementation/test gap that can be
198	  repaired by another automated cycle
199	- `review-too-large` — reviewer or readiness found the bead/result too broad for a
200	  bounded review cycle
201	- `review-error` — reviewer invocation failed, was malformed, overflowed, or
202	  returned no parseable verdict
203	- `review-manual-required` — reviewer failures or non-automatable findings
204	  exhausted automatic recovery and require operator action
205	- `triage-decomposed` — readiness or review decomposed a parent into child beads
206	- `triage-overflow` — decomposition reached the depth cap
207	- `triage-ambiguous` — readiness could not safely clarify the bead
208	- `auto-triage` — TriageContract: triage path mutated labels/status
209	
210	Candidate-cycle events (FEAT-010 candidate-cycle pipeline):
211	
212	- `candidate-pinned` — layer 2 pinned a candidate ref for the current cycle's
213	  `result_rev` before dispatching reviewers; body carries `candidate_ref`,
214	  `cycle_index`, and `attempt_id`
215	- `candidate-checks-failed` — post-run verification (tests, lint, gates) failed
216	  against the candidate; body carries check command and exit status
217	- `repair-cycle-started` — a repair cycle has been initiated in the still-live
218	  worktree; body carries `cycle_index`, `repair_context_from_review_group`, and
219	  `attempt_id`
220	- `repair-cycle-exhausted` — `repair_max_cycles` was reached for this attempt;
221	  bead moves to the `needs_human` lane
222	- `approved-land-conflict` — the candidate received unanimous `APPROVE` but merge
223	  to the base branch conflicted; bead is re-queued at its base revision without
224	  blame on the implementer or reviewer
225	- `final-result-landed` — the approved candidate has been merged to the base
226	  branch; immediately precedes the `closed-merged` lifecycle event
227	
228	Each event SHOULD include `kind`, `actor`, `created_at`, and a free-form
229	`body` (TD-004 schema). Drain events SHOULD additionally include the
230	`run-id` of the execution attempt in `body` or in a structured `extra`
231	field.
232	
233	## 5. Outcome → Label / Event / Extra Mapping
234	
235	When a drain attempt finishes, `execute-bead` returns one of a fixed set of
236	outcomes plus optional no_changes lifecycle evidence. The mapping below is the
237	canonical queue-management contract.
238	
239	Lifecycle reliability invariants:
240	
241	- Raw `no_changes` is attempt evidence, not a durable bead queue state. The
242	  drain loop MUST translate it into one of the rows below before mutating the
243	  bead.
244	- `execute-loop-retry-after` may be set only when retrying the same bead after
245	  time passes could plausibly succeed without human/spec/dependency changes.
246	- Every bead excluded from ordinary `ddx work` execution MUST have an
247	  explainable durable reason using existing mechanisms: dependency edge,
248	  `blocked` status, `needs_human`/triage labels, `execution-eligible=false`,
249	  `superseded-by`, epic/parent queue mode, or an active retry cooldown.
250	- `closed` means implementation, verification, and the default adversarial
251	  pre-close review gate have all passed, or the work was verified as already
252	  satisfied. Review failure never reopens a closed bead; it prevents close.
253	- Automatic implementation retry is bounded and applies only to classifications
254	  where the implementer had valid task context and further automated work can
255	  plausibly resolve the finding. Spec gaps, missing acceptance criteria,
256	  decomposition overflow, and exhausted reviewer failures require operator
257	  resolution.
258	- Latest terminal events and close evidence beat stale `execute-loop-*` Extra
259	  metadata. Reconciliation may clear stale management fields, but MUST preserve
260	  append-only events and evidence.
261	
262	| Outcome or lifecycle action | Status transition | Label changes | Events appended | Extra updates |
263	|---|---|---|---|---|
264	| `review_pass` after merged candidate | `in_progress → closed` | remove `claimed`, add `last-merged-rev:<sha>` (optional) | `review-pass` + `closed-merged` | `Extra["last-run"]`, `Extra["last-review"]`, and `Extra["closing_commit_sha"]` updated; clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields |
265	| `already_satisfied` | `in_progress → closed` | (none) | `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]`; clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields |
266	| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget remaining | no durable status change if continuing immediately; otherwise `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add no human label unless retry budget exhausted | `review-fixable-gap` + retry decision | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref; next cycle records `repair_context_from_review_group` |
267	| `review_fixable_gap` with retry budget exhausted | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref and exhausted budget |
268	| `review_spec_gap` / `review_missing_acceptance` | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human`, `triage:spec-gap` or `triage:missing-acceptance` | `review-block` | `Extra["last-review"]` carries findings ref |
269	| `review_too_large` | `in_progress → blocked` after children are filed, or `in_progress → blocked` with `needs-human-decomposition` at depth cap | add `decomposed` when children exist; otherwise add `needs_human` | `review-too-large` and optionally `triage-decomposed` / `triage-overflow` | `Extra["children"]` and AC mapping when decomposed |
270	| `review_error` below retry cap | no durable status change; retry reviewer for same `result_rev` | (none) | `review-error` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |
271	| `review_error` exhausted | `in_progress → blocked` | add `needs_human` | `review-manual-required` | `Extra["last-review-error"]` carries class, reviewer slot, and attempt count |
272	| `execution_failed` | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `unclaimed` + a structured failure event | `Extra["last-run"]` |
273	| verified no_changes already satisfied | `in_progress → closed` | remove no_changes triage labels if present | `no_changes_verified` + `closed-already-satisfied` | `Extra["last-run"]`; clear `execute-loop-retry-after`, `execute-loop-last-status`, and `execute-loop-last-detail` |
274	| unverified no_changes | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:no-changes-unverified` | `no_changes_unverified` | record verification command/result; do not set retry cooldown by default |
275	| unjustified no_changes / no rationale | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:no-changes-unjustified` | `no_changes_unjustified` | record rationale absence/detail; do not set retry cooldown by default |
276	| no_changes needs investigation because work is too large | `in_progress → blocked` after orchestrator child beads are filed, or `in_progress → blocked` with `needs-human-decomposition` at queue-level depth cap | add `decomposed` when children exist; add `needs_human` only for lossy/ambiguous/depth-overflow cases | `no_changes_needs_investigation` + `triage-decomposed` or `triage-overflow` | `Extra["last-rationale"]`, `Extra["children"]`, and AC mapping; do not set retry cooldown |
277	| no_changes needs investigation for non-decomposition reason | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | add `triage:needs-investigation`; add `needs_human` when operator action is required before retry | `no_changes_needs_investigation` | `Extra["last-rationale"]`; do not set retry cooldown by default |
278	| parent/epic/decomposed container | `in_progress → blocked` when children were created; otherwise `in_progress → open` with `execution-eligible=false` | add `decomposed` when children exist | `no_changes_decomposed` or `triage-decomposed` | `Extra["children"]` lists child IDs plus AC mapping, or `Extra["execution-eligible"]=false` explains container-only work |
279	| external blocker | `in_progress → blocked` for hard blockers, or `in_progress → open` with a `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` label for visible soft blockers | add `needs_human` or `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` | `no_changes_blocked` | `Extra["last-rationale"]` names the external blocker |
280	| superseded work | no terminal success; leave open only if visible history is needed | add no triage labels | structured superseded event if appended by caller | `Extra["superseded-by"]` names the replacement and makes ordinary execution ineligible |
281	| transient infra/quota/transport | `in_progress → open` (claim released) | (none) | `no_changes_recoverable`, `drain-paused-quota`, `rate-limit-retry`, or structured transport event | may set `execute-loop-retry-after` only for the retryable time-based condition |
282	| stale no_changes tracker metadata | no status change unless latest terminal evidence closes the bead | remove stale no_changes triage labels only when contradicted by terminal evidence | preserve historical events; append reconciliation event if performed | clear stale `execute-loop-*` management fields when latest terminal event or close evidence proves they are obsolete |
283	
284	`needs_human` is a **label**, not a status. It is the standard signal that
285	a human operator should look at the bead before drain re-attempts it.
286	
287	## 6. Claim Semantics
288	
289	Claim is metadata, not status. The persisted-status convention is that
290	`in_progress` implies a claim is held; `open` implies no claim. The actual
291	claim fields are:
292	
293	- `Extra["assignee"]` (string) — the entity holding the claim.
294	- `Extra["claimed-at"]` (RFC3339 timestamp) — when the claim was acquired.
295	- `Extra["claimed-pid"]` (int) — process id of the claim holder, advisory.
296	
297	Acquired:
298	
299	- During `open → in_progress` transition under the store lock.
300	- The drain loop and operator CLI (`ddx bead update --claim`) are the two
301	  legitimate drivers.
302	
303	Released:
304	
305	- Explicit `--unclaim` (or implicit unclaim on `in_progress → open`).
306	- On terminal close (`closed` / `cancelled`) the claim metadata is left in
307	  place as historical record but is no longer authoritative.
308	
309	Worker shutdown and interruption:
310	
311	- A worker that receives graceful shutdown while no bead attempt has reached a
312	  terminal mutation MUST release any active claim it owns before exiting. The
313	  bead returns `in_progress → open`, claim metadata is cleared, and an
314	  `unclaimed` event records `reason=worker_shutdown`.
315	- If the worker has already applied a terminal mutation (`closed`,
316	  `cancelled`, or `blocked`) for the current attempt, shutdown MUST NOT undo
317	  that terminal state. The worker may emit best-effort worker-disconnect
318	  telemetry, but bead state is already authoritative.
319	- If the child agent process or attempt worktree is interrupted by context
320	  cancellation, SIGTERM, SIGINT, or a server/operator cancel before a terminal
321	  mutation, the attempt is classified as mechanically disrupted. The worker
322	  preserves any available evidence, appends a structured attempt/interruption
323	  event, releases the claim, and leaves the bead re-claimable unless a separate
324	  explicit blocker or retryable cooldown was recorded.
325	- An ungraceful worker death may strand an `in_progress` bead temporarily. The
326	  stale-claim sweep is the recovery path: after the configured stale threshold,
327	  it releases the claim, appends the recovery event, and makes the bead
328	  queue-eligible again. This recovery MUST NOT delete the original attempt
329	  events or evidence bundle.
330	- Cooldown is not a shutdown-cleanup mechanism. A stopped or interrupted worker
331	  may set `execute-loop-retry-after` only when the recorded outcome is a
332	  retryable time-based condition; ordinary shutdown, SIGTERM/SIGINT, or
333	  operator cancel releases the claim without parking the bead behind time.
334	
335	Stale claim handling (TriageContract owns the policy):
336	
337	- A claim is *stale* if `claimed-at` is older than the configured stale
338	  threshold AND the bead is still `in_progress`.
339	- Stale claims are released by the auto-triage path (`auto-triage` event),
340	  status moves `in_progress → open`, and the bead becomes queue-eligible
341	  again.
342	- Auto-triage MUST NOT delete prior claim metadata; it appends an event
343	  recording the release.
344	
345	## 7. Naming-Role Decision Matrix
346	
347	Every name observed in code, schema, docs, or persisted data is assigned
348	a single category here. Names not in the persisted-status set MUST NOT
349	appear as `status` values.
350	
351	| Name | Category | Rationale |
352	|---|---|---|
353	| `open` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; queue-eligible default. |
354	| `in_progress` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; implies an active claim. |
355	| `closed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-success path. |
356	| `blocked` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; hard precondition unmet. |
357	| `proposed` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; pre-triage backlog. |
358	| `cancelled` | Persisted status | bd/br canonical; terminal-abandoned path. |
359	| `done` | Removed alias | Historical alias of `closed` in code paths. Not a persisted status. The reconciliation sibling bead replaces remaining code references with `closed`. |
360	| `pending` | Derived queue category | Used by queue derivation to mean "open AND has unmet deps" (a synonym for unsatisfied dependencies). Never persisted. The reconciliation sibling bead either renames it to `waiting` or folds it into `blocked` derivation. |
361	| `ready` | Derived queue category | "open AND no unmet deps AND not claimed". Computed from status+deps+claim; never persisted. |
362	| `review` | Terminal phase / event | A *phase of work*, not a status. Implemented as the `review-block` / `review-pass` event pair plus the `needs_human` label. Never persisted as a status. |
363	| `needs_human` | Label | Signals "an operator must intervene". Stays a label per bd/br compatibility — never a status. |
364	| `needs_investigation` | Label | Signals "the cause is unclear; triage required". Stays a **label** per bd/br compatibility. The reconciliation sibling bead removes any code path that treats it as a status. |
365	| `blocked-on-upstream:<id>` | Label | Parameterized label naming the upstream blocker. Distinct from the `blocked` status: a bead can carry the label while remaining `open` for visibility, or while `blocked` for enforcement. |
366	| `decomposed` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_decomposed`; pairs with `Extra["children"]`. |
367	| `triage` | Label | Set by drain on `no_changes_no_evidence` or by auto-triage. |
368	| `idle` / `draining` / `paused-quota` / `paused-rate-limit` / `exiting` | Worker state | Lives in the drain loop process, not on the bead. See section 10. |
369	
370	## 8. Per-Hygiene-Bead Contracts
371	
372	Each hygiene bead's AC must cite this section. Anything not in the
373	contract here is out of scope for that bead.
374	
375	### 8.1 NoChangesContract (ddx-b24e9630)
376	
377	NoChangesContract outcomes use the canonical mapping in section 5. This section
378	exists only to bind the hygiene bead to that mapping; it does not define a
379	second disposition table.
380	
381	Claim behavior: the claim is released for every no_changes action that leaves
382	the bead `open`. It is not released separately when the same mutation moves the
383	bead directly to terminal `closed` or hard `blocked`.
384	
385	Loop interaction: the try package parses and verifies no_changes rationale,
386	then returns the lifecycle action. The drain loop applies section 5 exactly and
387	appends one of the no_changes event kinds from section 4; it does not invent
388	new event kinds or use cooldown as a generic parking lot.
389	
390	### 8.2 Bead Readiness Assessment And Triage Contract (ddx-3c154349)
391	
392	Bead readiness assessment is the canonical pre-claim decision for
393	actionability and scope. It owns the readiness queue decision and runs before a
394	worker owns the bead, so most readiness actions start from `open`.
395	Lint/rubric scoring is the diagnostic pass inside readiness, and post-attempt
396	triage is a separate after-evidence queue action. The implementation entrypoint
397	may still be named `MODE: intake` for compatibility, but that is legacy
398	wording only; the product concept is bead readiness assessment.
399	
400	Status transitions used:
401	
402	- `in_progress → open` when releasing stale claims.
403	- `open → blocked` when triage decides a bead has an unmet hard
404	  precondition.
405	- `open → blocked` when readiness decomposes a parent, reaches decomposition depth
406	  overflow, or finds ambiguity that cannot be safely rewritten.
407	
408	Labels added: `triage`, `needs_human`, `blocked-on-upstream:<id>`,
409	`decomposed`, `needs-human-decomposition`, `triage:spec-gap`,
410	`triage:missing-acceptance` (as applicable).
411	
412	Labels removed: `triage` after a triaged bead is reclaimed cleanly (the
413	reverse mutation of `triage` is "next successful claim removes the
414	label").
415	
416	Events fired: `auto-triage`, `triage-ambiguous`, `triage-decomposed`,
417	`triage-overflow`, `unclaimed`, optionally `blocked`.
418	
419	Claim behavior: the auto-triage sweep is the only path that releases a
420	claim it does not own. It MUST log the prior `assignee` and `claimed-at`
421	in the appended event body.
422	
423	Loop interaction: readiness and triage run out-of-band of execute-bead. They
424	MUST NOT race with an active drain attempt holding the store lock — they
425	acquire the same store lock and releases-then-reclaims is not their job.
426	
427	### 8.3 QuotaPauseContract (ddx-aede917d)
428	
429	Status transitions used: none. Quota is a worker-state concern, not a
430	bead-state concern.
431	
432	Labels: none added on the affected bead. The current attempt is treated
433	as `execution_failed` (claim released, status returns to `open`).
434	
435	Events fired: `drain-paused-quota` is appended to a worker-scoped event
436	log (drain-process record), not to the bead's event stream. A
437	`unclaimed` event is appended to the bead.
438	
439	Claim behavior: the claim on the in-flight bead is released cleanly so
440	another worker (or a later resumed worker) can pick it up.
441	
442	Loop interaction: the worker transitions to worker-state
443	`paused-quota` (section 10). It stops claiming new beads until the
444	configured backoff elapses or an explicit resume signal arrives.
445	
446	### 8.4 RateLimitRetryContract (ddx-c6e3db02)
447	
448	Status transitions used: none. Rate-limit retry is internal to a single
449	attempt; the bead remains `in_progress` throughout.
450	
451	Labels: none.
452	
453	Events fired: `rate-limit-retry` per retry, on the bead's event stream.
454	Each event carries the retry count and the wait duration in the body.
455	
456	Claim behavior: claim is held continuously across retries.
457	
458	Loop interaction: rate-limit handling is bounded by the retry budget; on
459	budget exhaustion the outcome becomes `execution_failed` and the
460	standard mapping in section 5 applies. The worker briefly enters
461	`paused-rate-limit` for the wait window, then returns to `draining`.
462	
463	### 8.5 LockContentionContract (ddx-da11a34a)
464	
465	Status transitions used: none. Lock contention is a store-access
466	concern, not a bead-state concern.
467	
468	Labels: none.
469	
470	Events fired: `lock-contention` (append-only, on the affected bead, only
471	after the contention has been resolved and the mutation succeeded — so a
472	future audit can see that contention was observed). Pure read-side
473	contention does not emit an event.
474	
475	Claim behavior: unaffected. The claim is acquired or not; partial states
476	are not persisted.
477	
478	Loop interaction: the worker retries the locked operation with
479	exponential backoff up to a bounded budget. On budget exhaustion the
480	calling outcome maps to `execution_failed` (section 5). The worker does
481	not enter a dedicated worker-state for lock contention; it remains
482	`draining` and treats the failure as ordinary.
483	
484	## 9. Future-Change Process
485	
486	This is the rule of record for any subsequent change to bead state.
487	
488	> Any bead that introduces or changes a label, an event kind, an outcome →
489	> state mapping, claim handling, or worker-state semantics MUST cite the
490	> TD-031 section that authorizes the change. If no section authorizes it,
491	> TD-031 is amended in the same PR (or a parent bead) before the dependent
492	> work lands.
493	>
494	> Adding a new persisted bead status is **not** authorized by TD-031.
495	> It requires upstream bd/br coordination AND an amendment to ADR-004.
496	
497	In practice:
498	
499	- A new event kind: amend section 4 in the same PR.
500	- A new outcome → state mapping: amend section 5.
501	- A new label: amend section 7.
502	- A new worker state: amend section 10.
503	- A new persisted status: do not start the work; file an ADR-004
504	  amendment first.
505	
506	CI guard: a sibling bead adds a check that any change to
507	`bead-record.schema.json` or to the persisted-status enum touches
508	ADR-004 and TD-031 in the same commit. That guard is out of scope for
509	this bead.
510	
511	## 10. Worker-State Enumeration
512	
513	Worker state is the in-process state of the drain loop. It is **distinct
514	from bead state** and is not persisted in the bead store. It exists only
515	for the worker's lifetime.
516	
517	| Worker state | Meaning | Entry | Exit |
518	|---|---|---|---|
519	| `idle` | Worker is up but not actively claiming. | Worker startup; transient between attempts. | Operator or scheduler moves it to `draining`. |
520	| `draining` | Worker is actively claiming and executing beads. | Default operating state. | Quota or rate-limit pause; explicit stop. |
521	| `paused-quota` | Worker has observed quota exhaustion on its harness. | `drain-paused-quota` event. | `drain-resumed-quota` event after backoff or operator resume. |
522	| `paused-rate-limit` | Worker is sleeping out a rate-limit retry window inside an attempt. | Rate-limit response from harness. | Wait window elapses; worker resumes the same attempt. |
523	| `exiting` | Worker is shutting down cleanly; will not claim more beads. | Operator stop or terminal failure. | Process exit. |
524	
525	Worker state is observable via the worker's stdout/log; it is not stored
526	on any bead. Hygiene beads that affect worker state (QuotaPauseContract,
527	RateLimitRetryContract) MUST NOT add new fields to the bead schema to
528	represent these transient states.
529	
530	## 11. Migration Plan (placeholder)
531	
532	The actual migration survey — which existing beads carry orphaned status
533	values, which code paths persist non-canonical statuses, and what the
534	mechanical rename looks like — runs in the sibling reconciliation bead.
535	This TD does not catalogue current data. The readiness assessment and triage
536	queue-action contract in section 8.2 is already defined here; only
537	compatibility wording such as `MODE: intake` remains legacy.
538	
539	Sibling beads handling the migration:
540	
541	- Schema/docs/code reconciliation: align FEAT-004 line 65 with the
542	  schema; rename code references to non-status names; add CI guards.
543	- Hygiene-bead AC substitution: replace each hygiene bead's
544	  contract section with a reference to TD-031 §8.x.
545	- Migration survey: scan persisted bead stores in known DDx projects for
546	  non-canonical status values and produce a remediation list.
547	
548	When those siblings land, this section is replaced with a concrete
549	migration record citing the sibling bead IDs and the survey result.

11	# Feature: Prompt Evidence Assembly and Size Invariants
12	
13	**ID:** FEAT-022
14	**Status:** Implemented
15	**Priority:** P1
16	**Owner:** DDx Team
17	
18	## Overview
19	
20	DDx constructs prompts for LLM agents from multiple evidence sources: bead
21	metadata, governing documents, git diffs, comparison-arm outputs, user-supplied
22	prompt files, and inline request bodies. Today these assembly paths inline
23	content without size bounds, produce oversize prompts, and in the reviewer's
24	case trigger silent provider-side failures that cascade into redone primary
25	work and wasted tokens.
26	
27	This feature establishes a single repo-wide invariant: **no prompt DDx
28	constructs or accepts may have unbounded size.** It specifies the shared
29	primitives every prompt-building call site must use, the two supported
30	assembly strategies, the failure semantics when inputs exceed caps, and the
31	telemetry needed to observe the system.
32	
33	## Problem Statement
34	
35	**Current situation:**
36	
37	- The post-merge bead reviewer (`BuildReviewPrompt`) inlines full governing
38	  documents and full `git show` diffs without a cap. Large diffs push the
39	  prompt past the reviewer model's context window; some providers return a
40	  hard error, some return zero bytes. DDx surfaces these as `review-error`,
41	  fails to close the bead, and the next worker redoes the primary task —
42	  burning the same large token budget 3–4 times per bead before an operator
43	  intervenes.
44	- The comparison grader (`buildGradingPrompt`) has the same failure shape:
45	  per-arm outputs and diffs are inlined without a cap.
46	- A second review-prompt assembler lives in `cmd/bead_review.go` as
47	  duplicated code, with divergent escape semantics from the agent-side
48	  assembler.
49	- Five call sites (`runner.go`, `compare_adapter.go:181`,
50	  `compare_adapter.go:614`, `service_run.go:83`, `execute_bead.go:1291`)
51	  read `--prompt` files with `os.ReadFile` and no cap. A 100 MB prompt file
52	  reaches the provider unchecked.
53	- Three inline prompt-ingress paths (`/api/agent/run` body `Prompt`,
54	  `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` env in exec definitions, docgraph prompt metadata)
55	  accept prompt bodies with no cap.
56	- Server text egress (MCP, REST, GraphQL) returns full document, diff,
57	  session, and persona payloads without caps. Any of these responses may
58	  later flow back into a prompt.
59	- No DDx call site has section-level telemetry on prompt assembly. When
60	  reviews fail, diagnosis today requires per-execution bundle archaeology.
61	
62	**Desired outcome:** Every LLM prompt DDx constructs, accepts, or emits
63	through text-returning server endpoints is bounded by explicit caps.
64	Prompt-assembly call sites use shared primitives that enforce caps with
65	clear truncation markers. Oversize inputs fail fast with actionable errors
66	rather than silently corrupting provider calls. Operators see per-section
67	byte accounting on every review and grading attempt.
68	
69	## Requirements
70	
71	### Functional
72	
73	**Shared evidence primitives**
74	
75	1. **Single evidence-assembly package.** DDx provides one package exporting
76	   the primitives every prompt-building call site must use. The package
77	   covers, at minimum, these capabilities:
78	   - Byte-size caps for inlined files, diffs, governing documents, and
79	     total prompts, each with per-harness override resolution.
80	   - Clamped file read (returns content plus amount truncated).
81	   - Clamped text output (bounded length with a canonical truncation
82	     marker).
83	   - Section fitting under a total byte budget with priority-ordered
84	     inclusion. Default behavior is line-based and type-agnostic;
85	     markdown-aware heading extraction is an optional secondary mode.
86	   - Diff clamping and diff decomposition. The decomposer returns file
87	     inventory, per-file stat lines, and hunk headers so large diffs
88	     can degrade to stat + hunk-headers only.
89	   - Two strategy helpers, one for inline-with-cap assembly and one for
90	     ref-only metadata assembly (see §2).
91	   - A canonical truncation-marker string so downstream parsers
92	     recognize truncation uniformly across call sites.
93	
94	   Specific Go identifiers, type shapes, and constant values are defined
95	   in the solution design document for this feature, not in this spec.
96	
97	1a. **Cap defaults and configurability.** Byte-size caps have conservative
98	    defaults shipped in the binary. Project-level overrides are expressed
99	    in `.ddx/config.yaml` under a dedicated evidence-caps section;
100	    per-harness overrides resolve from the same configuration file.
101	    Defaults and override precedence are defined in the solution design
102	    for this feature. The invariant is that every cap is configurable at
103	    the project level without a rebuild; specific numeric defaults are
104	    not frozen by this spec.
105	
106	2. **Two supported assembly strategies.** Each call site must adopt one:
107	   - **Ref-only metadata** — the primary bead prompt's pattern. Paths and
108	     titles only; agent reads file content via its own tools. Appropriate
109	     when the agent has filesystem access and the prompt does not need to
110	     guarantee the agent saw specific content.
111	   - **Inline-with-cap** — reviewer and grader's pattern. Content is
112	     inlined with bounded size, priority-ordered sections, and truncation
113	     markers. Appropriate when the call must guarantee the agent saw
114	     specific evidence.
115	
116	2a. **Bounded evidence bundle is canonical; same-worktree access is
117	    supplemental.** When the review gate runs in the still-live attempt
118	    worktree (per the FEAT-010 candidate-cycle pipeline), the bounded evidence
119	    bundle assembled by this feature remains the canonical and sufficient review
120	    input. Read-only same-worktree tool access (file reads, directory searches)
121	    is supplemental: it may provide additional context but does not substitute
122	    for or override the bounded bundle. This invariant applies regardless of
123	    whether the reviewer's working directory is the attempt worktree. The bounded
124	    bundle alone must be sufficient for a verdict; any finding that requires
125	    same-worktree access to reach is advisory, not a gate requirement.
126	
127	3. **Repo-wide invariant (no unbounded prompts).** No code path may produce
128	   a value that ends up as an agent-invocation prompt, as a text response
129	   from a server handler that carries library, execution, diff, session,
130	   or persona content, or as an inline prompt body accepted through any
131	   ingress surface, without going through an evidence-package primitive.
132	   Exceptions require an explicit `evidence:allow-unbounded` source
133	   annotation with a justifying comment and are enforced by lint (see
134	   Non-Functional §Lint). Static/literal prompt fragments compiled into
135	   the binary are exempt; the invariant governs dynamic assembly from
136	   runtime inputs, not constant strings.
137	
138	3a. **Static-prompt minimum-prompt rule.** Every static instruction string
139	    DDx embeds in an LLM prompt MUST declare its load-bearing guardrails
140	    inline as a Go comment list above the constant, one bullet per
141	    guardrail with a pointer to the failure mode it prevents. Edits MUST
142	    preserve every listed guardrail. New guardrails MUST be added to the
143	    list. A regression test MUST assert each listed guardrail still
144	    appears in the rendered prompt. This rule applies to STATIC prompt
145	    bodies; dynamic evidence egress is covered by the §5–§7 cap
146	    primitives and is unaffected.
147	
148	**Bounded prompt construction**
149	
150	4. **Single review assembler.** Review prompts are assembled by exactly one
151	   function (`agent.BuildReviewPrompt`). Any duplicate review-prompt
152	   assembler in the CLI command layer is collapsed into this function.
153	   Divergent escape, instruction-template, or section-ordering logic is
154	   reconciled with byte-equivalence tests before the duplicate is removed.
155	
156	5. **Review prompt contents.** The review prompt includes, in priority
157	   order:
158	   1. Bead metadata + acceptance criteria + bead notes (minimum evidence
159	      floor — always present regardless of budget).
160	   2. Changed-file inventory (names + stat lines — minimum evidence floor).
161	   3. Governing document content, bounded per §1. Governing docs that
162	      exceed their cap degrade to heading outline + first N lines.
163	   4. Diff, ranked by relevance (files named in AC text > files matching
164	      governing ref paths > others); per-file cap applies; large files
165	      degrade to stat + hunk headers.
166	
167	6. **Grading prompt contents.** The grading prompt follows the same
168	   bounded-assembly pattern and additionally includes, bounded via
169	   `ClampOutput`, each comparison arm's `PostRunOut`, `PostRunOK`, and
170	   `ToolCalls`. These are first-class evidence fields on the comparison
171	   arm type and must not be silently dropped.
172	
173	7. **Pre-dispatch short-circuit.** When a bounded assembly still exceeds
174	   `MaxPromptBytes` after all trimming, the call site must not dispatch
175	   the provider. Reviewer paths emit a distinct
176	   `review-error: context_overflow` outcome (see §13).
177	
178	**Bounded prompt ingress**
179	
180	8. **Hard-fail on oversize `--prompt` files.** Every call site that reads a
181	   user-supplied prompt file uses `evidence.ReadFileClamped(MaxPromptBytes)`
182	   and hard-fails on oversize with an actionable error message that
183	   includes observed size and cap. Silent truncation is forbidden — a
184	   user-supplied prompt truncated without notice produces incorrect
185	   results the user cannot diagnose.
186	
187	9. **Hard-fail on oversize inline prompt bodies.** The same hard-fail
188	   applies to inline prompt bodies in `/api/agent/run` request bodies,
189	   `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` environment values, and any other ingress path that
190	   accepts a raw prompt string. Size is checked against `MaxPromptBytes`
191	   before the prompt is handed to the agent.
192	
193	**Bounded server text egress**
194	
195	10. **Universal bounded text responses.** Every server text-returning
196	    handler — MCP tools, REST endpoints, GraphQL resolvers — that emits
197	    library document content, execution bundle content, diff content,
198	    session content, or persona content applies `ClampOutput` and marks
199	    the response as truncated in the response structure when trimming
200	    occurred. The MCP tool response structure, the REST JSON response,
201	    and the GraphQL resolver return type each carry an explicit
202	    `truncated: bool` and `original_bytes` field where applicable.
203	
204	11. **No prompt-adjacent surface is unbounded.** The invariant applies
205	    regardless of whether a specific response is, today, actually consumed
206	    back into a prompt. A bounded-by-default policy prevents new
207	    consumers from reintroducing the overflow class.
208	
209	**Error classes and review policy**
210	
211	12. **Adversarial review evidence contract.** The default close gate assembles
212	    one bounded evidence bundle per `result_rev` and dispatches two no-tool
213	    reviewer invocations. Each reviewer must grade every acceptance criterion
214	    with evidence. An `APPROVE` without per-AC evidence is treated as
215	    `review-error: unparseable`, not as a pass.
216	
217	13. **Review error taxonomy.** The reviewer outcome event distinguishes
218	    four classes:
219	    - `review-error: context_overflow` — bounded assembly exceeded
220	      `MaxPromptBytes` post-trim; provider was not called.
221	    - `review-error: provider_empty` — provider returned zero bytes.
222	    - `review-error: unparseable` — provider returned text but no
223	      recognizable verdict line.
224	    - `review-error: transport` — network or provider error.
225	
226	14. **Reviewer failure preserves the gate.** Reviewer failure must not
227	    close the bead. This invariant (enforced today by
228	    `execute_bead_review_failure_modes_test.go`) is preserved: bounded
229	    prompts change how the reviewer is called, not whether a failed
230	    review can auto-approve.
231	
232	15. **Bounded review retry.** Reviewer invocations are capped at a
233	    configurable N attempts per `result_rev`. On the Nth failure, DDx
234	    emits a terminal `review-manual-required` event, blocks the bead for
235	    operator review, and stops review retries. A new `result_rev` (fresh primary
236	    attempt after manual operator action) resets the counter. Iterations that do not
237	    produce a `result_rev` (e.g. `--no-merge` runs, execution-failed
238	    outcomes) do not consume the review-retry budget; the counter is
239	    scoped strictly to reviewer attempts against a committed result.
240	    This bounds the cost-bleed pattern observed in the filed bug (same
241	    primary task retried 3–4 times) without weakening the gate.
242	
243	**Telemetry and observability**
244	
245	16. **Section-level assembly telemetry.** Review and grading paths
246	    extend the execute-bead attempt bundle (FEAT-014 §19/§20, FEAT-005
247	    §Execute-Bead Evidence Bundle) with an evidence-assembly block
248	    containing:
249	    - per-section bytes (bead / governing / diff / arm-output)
250	    - list of selected vs omitted governing refs
251	    - list of selected vs omitted diff files
252	    - truncation reasons per section
253	    - total `input_bytes` and `output_bytes`
254	    - `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, `model`
255	
256	    Runtime metrics fields already defined by FEAT-014 (`harness`,
257	    `model`, `session_id`, `elapsed_ms`, `input_tokens`,
258	    `output_tokens`, `total_tokens`, `cost_usd`, `base_rev`,
259	    `result_rev`) are not duplicated by FEAT-022; evidence-assembly
260	    telemetry is additive to that record, not a replacement.
261	
262	17. **Compact event summary.** `review`, `review-error`, and
263	    `compare-result` event bodies carry a compact summary
264	    (`input_bytes`, `output_bytes`, `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, `model`).
265	    Full section detail stays in artifacts; event bodies respect the
266	    existing size cap on bead event bodies.
267	
268	17. **Metrics surface.** DDx's existing review-outcomes metrics surface
269	    exposes prompt-size quantiles and the four-class failure breakdown
270	    defined in §12. The specific CLI and API entry points that carry
271	    this data are inherited from FEAT-006 and FEAT-014; FEAT-022 adds
272	    fields, not new commands.
273	
274	18. **Structured assembly topology.** The multi-turn structured-evidence
275	    assembly architecture is specified in TD-033. FEAT-022 owns the
276	    bounded prompt invariants, section ordering, and failure semantics;
277	    TD-033 owns the caller-facing structured evidence envelope and the
278	    no-tool reviewer-mode convention used by FEAT-006 and FEAT-008.
279	
280	### Non-Functional
281	
282	- **Lint (structural sink coverage).** A vet/lint rule, scoped
283	  structurally not by dataflow, enforces the invariant in §3. Rule keys:
284	  - assignment to `RunOptions.Prompt`
285	  - construction of `mcpContent{Type:"text", ...}` literals
286	  - server handlers that write text response bodies from library,
287	    execution, diff, session, or persona data
288	  - readers of `opts.PromptFile` or inline prompt-body request fields
289	  Any flagged path must route through an `internal/evidence` primitive
290	  or carry an explicit `evidence:allow-unbounded` annotation with a
291	  justifying comment.
292	
293	- **Byte-based enforcement and calibration.** All caps are enforced in
294	  bytes. Token-based caps are deferred until DDx has a per-harness
295	  tokenizer binding. Default byte caps are calibrated conservatively
296	  relative to the smallest common-reviewer model's context window using
297	  an assumed worst-case bytes-per-token ratio; the solution design
298	  document carries the concrete calibration table and the per-harness
299	  override mechanism (see §1a). If runtime telemetry (§15) shows that
300	  the default is either rejecting valid prompts or failing to prevent
301	  provider-side overflow, the cap is retuned via configuration, not
302	  via a rebuild.
303	
304	- **Failure mode clarity.** Oversize inputs produce errors that name the
305	  input source, the observed size, and the cap. Empty provider responses
306	  are classified distinctly from unparseable responses (see §12).
307	
308	- **Backward compatibility.** Bead 0 primitives ship with permissive
309	  default caps and cause no behavioral change on land. Tightening caps
310	  and consuming the primitives happens in subsequent beads; each
311	  consumer change is independently reviewable.
312	
313	- **Performance.** Assembly cost per call is bounded by total prompt
314	  size (`O(MaxPromptBytes)`), not by input size on disk. Clamping
315	  occurs at read time; oversize files are not fully loaded into memory.
316	
317	## User Stories
318	
319	### US-220: Pre-close reviewer succeeds on large changes without silent overflow
320	**As** an operator running `ddx work` against beads with large diffs
321	**I want** the default adversarial pre-close reviewers to produce verdicts on
322	every acceptable diff, and to fail with an actionable overflow error otherwise
323	**So that** primary work is not silently redone after empty reviewer
324	responses
325	
326	**Acceptance Criteria:**
327	- Given a bead whose diff plus governing docs would exceed
328	  `MaxPromptBytes`, when the reviewer runs, then the assembler trims
329	  per §5 and the reviewer sees a bounded prompt
330	- Given trimming cannot reduce the prompt below `MaxPromptBytes`, when
331	  the reviewer runs, then the provider is not called and the bead
332	  receives a `review-error: context_overflow` event with observed and
333	  cap sizes
334	- Given a reviewer invocation emits a recognized verdict, when the
335	  outcome is recorded, then the event body carries `input_bytes`,
336	  `output_bytes`, `elapsed_ms`, `harness`, and `model`
337	
338	### US-221: Operator distinguishes reviewer-failure classes
339	**As** an operator diagnosing a wave of reviewer failures
340	**I want** to distinguish context overflow, empty provider output,
341	unparseable output, and transport errors
342	**So that** I know whether to tune prompt caps, retry a transient
343	failure, or escalate a provider bug
344	
345	**Acceptance Criteria:**
346	- Given a reviewer produces zero bytes of output, then the event
347	  summary classifies the outcome as `review-error: provider_empty`
348	- Given a reviewer produces text that lacks a verdict line, then the
349	  event summary classifies the outcome as `review-error: unparseable`
350	- Given `ddx agent metrics review-outcomes` is queried, then results
351	  include counts per error class and prompt-size quantiles
352	
353	### US-222: Reviewer failure has a bounded retry ceiling
354	**As** an operator running `ddx work` overnight
355	**I want** reviewer failures on the same `result_rev` to stop
356	re-executing primary after a small number of attempts
357	**So that** a single broken bead cannot consume arbitrary tokens
358	
359	**Acceptance Criteria:**
360	- Given a bead's most recent `result_rev` has accumulated N reviewer
361	  failures, when the next worker evaluates it, then the bead does not
362	  re-execute primary and emits a terminal `review-manual-required`
363	  event
364	- Given a `review-manual-required` event exists, when an operator
365	  lists blocked beads, then the bead appears there with the triggering
366	  failure class and is not closed
367	
368	### US-223: Oversize `--prompt` files fail fast with a clear error
369	**As** a developer running `ddx run --prompt ./huge.md`
370	**I want** DDx to reject the oversize file with an actionable error
371	**So that** I don't silently send a multi-megabyte prompt to a provider
372	
373	**Acceptance Criteria:**
374	- Given `--prompt path` points at a file exceeding `MaxPromptBytes`,
375	  when the command runs, then it exits non-zero with an error naming
376	  the file, observed size, and cap
377	- Given the same file is supplied to `ddx run` server-side via the
378	  run invocation API, then the API returns a 4xx error with the same
379	  information in the response body
380	- Given `DDX_AGENT_PROMPT` in an exec definition exceeds
381	  `MaxPromptBytes`, then the exec fails at startup with the same error
382	
383	### US-224: Server text responses never exceed configured bounds
384	**As** a client consuming MCP, REST, or GraphQL responses that may
385	later be reused as prompt context
386	**I want** every response that inlines library, execution, diff,
387	session, or persona content to be bounded
388	**So that** response consumers cannot accidentally reintroduce the
389	overflow class
390	
391	**Acceptance Criteria:**
392	- Given an MCP `read_document` call targets a file exceeding
393	  `MaxInlinedFileBytes`, then the response carries a truncated
394	  payload plus `truncated: true` and `original_bytes`
395	- Given a GraphQL `DocumentByPath` query returns content, then the
396	  same truncation contract applies
397	- Given a REST handler returns execution detail, session content,
398	  worker prompt, or diff content, then the same contract applies
399	
400	### US-225: Review and grading prompts carry full minimum evidence
401	**As** an implementer reviewing a failed bead
402	**I want** the reviewer to always see bead acceptance, notes, and the
403	full list of changed files, even under heavy trimming
404	**So that** small surface-level issues are never missed due to
405	truncation
406	
407	**Acceptance Criteria:**
408	- Given any bounded review assembly, then the assembled prompt
409	  contains the bead's `Title`, `Description`, `Acceptance`, `Notes`,
410	  and the full list of changed file names with stat lines
411	- Given a bounded grading assembly, then the assembled prompt
412	  contains each arm's `Output`, `PostRunOut`, `PostRunOK`, and
413	  `ToolCalls` after output clamping but never as omitted-entirely
414	
415	### US-226: Unannotated unbounded prompt sinks fail CI
416	**As** a maintainer of DDx
417	**I want** any new code path that writes into a prompt sink without
418	going through the shared evidence primitives to be rejected by CI
419	**So that** the no-unbounded-prompts invariant (§3) cannot decay as
420	the codebase grows
421	
422	**Acceptance Criteria:**
423	- Given a change introduces a direct file read whose output flows into
424	  an agent prompt without going through a shared evidence primitive,
425	  when CI runs the repository lint, then the lint exits non-zero and
426	  names the offending file and line
427	- Given a change introduces a server text-response handler that
428	  inlines library, execution, diff, session, or persona content
429	  without bounding the payload, when CI runs the repository lint,
430	  then the lint exits non-zero with the same reporting
431	- Given an intentional exception is required, when the code path
432	  carries an `evidence:allow-unbounded` annotation with a non-empty
433	  justifying comment, then the lint accepts the path
434	
435	### US-227: Duplicate review assemblers are collapsed without drift
436	**As** a maintainer responsible for keeping review behavior consistent
437	between CLI and automated invocations
438	**I want** the two historical review-prompt assemblers collapsed into
439	one, with a gating test that proves byte-equivalence before the
440	duplicate is deleted
441	**So that** behavior does not silently drift between the CLI and the
442	execute-loop review path
443	
444	**Acceptance Criteria:**
445	- Given a fixture bead plus a fixture result revision plus fixture
446	  governing refs, when both the pre-collapse and post-collapse code
447	  paths assemble a review prompt for the same inputs, then the
448	  resulting prompt bytes are identical
449	- Given the collapse is complete, when the repository is grepped for
450	  the duplicate assembler's function name and its helper symbols,
451	  then zero matches remain
452	
453	## Dependencies
454	
455	- **FEAT-002** (DDx Server) — `/api/agent/run`, `/api/providers`,
456	  `/api/executions`, and the MCP/GraphQL surfaces are the server-side
457	  text sinks governed by the §10 bounded-egress requirement. FEAT-002
458	  owns the response-shape definitions; FEAT-022 adds the `truncated`
459	  and `original_bytes` fields to those shapes and expects FEAT-002 to
460	  reciprocate the field-definition reference.
461	- **FEAT-005** (Artifacts) — the execute-bead attempt bundle structure
462	  that FEAT-022 §15 extends with an evidence-assembly block.
463	- **FEAT-006** (Agent Service) — review, grading, execute-bead, and
464	  session capture are owned here. FEAT-022 adds requirements on how
465	  the evidence these paths carry is assembled without re-owning the
466	  paths themselves.
467	- **TD-033** (Multi-Turn Structured Evidence Assembly) — documents the
468	  caller-facing assembly envelope and the no-tool reviewer-mode
469	  convention used by FEAT-006 and FEAT-008.
470	- **FEAT-014** (Token Awareness) — owns the `result.json` runtime
471	  metrics schema (§19/§20 of FEAT-014). FEAT-022 §15 is additive to
472	  that schema; FEAT-022 does not redefine the runtime metrics fields
473	  FEAT-014 already governs.
474	
475	## Delivery Sequencing
476	
477	FEAT-022 is delivered in incremental stages. The specific tracker
478	beads that implement each stage are maintained in the beads tracker,
479	not in this spec; the stages below describe the *sequencing
480	constraints*, not the bead breakdown.
481	
482	### Stage A — Shared primitives and structural sink lint
483	Lands requirement §1, §1a, and the lint rule under Non-Functional
484	§Lint. Ships with permissive default caps so no existing behavior
485	changes on land. Blocks all subsequent stages.
486	
487	### Stage B — Collapse duplicate review assembler
488	Lands requirement §4. Byte-equivalence test (US-227) gates the
489	deletion of the duplicate. Blocks Stage C for the review path.
490	
491	### Stage C — Bounded review and grading assembly
492	Lands requirements §5, §6, §7. The review and grading paths are
493	independently shippable after Stage B completes for review; the
494	grading path has no dependency on Stage B.
495	
496	### Stage D — Hard-fail on oversize prompt ingress
497	Lands requirements §8 and §9 across every `--prompt` file site and
498	every inline-ingress surface. Depends only on Stage A.
499	
500	### Stage E — Universal bounded server text egress
501	Lands requirements §10 and §11 across MCP, REST, and GraphQL
502	handlers. Depends only on Stage A.
503	
504	### Stage F — Section-level telemetry
505	Lands requirements §15, §16, §17. Depends on Stage C for the
506	evidence-assembly block shape.
507	
508	### Stage G — Error-class distinction and bounded review retry
509	Lands requirements §12, §13, §14. Depends on Stage C for the
510	context-overflow outcome class.
511	
512	## Out of Scope
513	
514	- A repo-wide `EvidenceAssembler` struct that every caller instantiates
515	  and drives. Primitives are shared; the top-level assembly order
516	  differs per caller (review, grading, primary) and forcing unification
517	  would couple unrelated call sites.
518	- Token-based caps. Deferred until a per-harness tokenizer binding
519	  exists; byte caps are the enforcement unit.
520	- Map-reduce per-file review (small per-file reviews + an aggregator).
521	  Noted as a potential follow-on if bounded assembly plus diff caps
522	  proves insufficient; not in scope here.
523	- `FEAT-*` section anchoring for governing documents. Requires new
524	  `spec-id` syntax; separate effort.
525	- JSON reviewer output contract. Orthogonal to size management; the
526	  existing markdown-with-strict-parse contract is adequate for the
527	  current failure modes.
528	- Mirroring review-style telemetry onto primary execute-bead events.
529	  Primary already has `result.json` / `usage.json` artifacts and the
530	  symmetry would add noise.
531	- Extending the invariant to non-prompt-adjacent server responses
532	  (e.g. metadata-only endpoints). The invariant is scoped to text
533	  content that could plausibly become prompt input.
534
